does: This paper develops a toy model to analyze and unpack the concept of interference weights in neural networks. It provides a simplified mathematical framework for understanding how weight interference phenomena manifest in small-scale networks, using concrete examples and analysis to isolate the key mechanisms. The work demonstrates how different components of learned weights can interfere with each other during training and inference, and characterizes the conditions under which this interference becomes pronounced or benign. Through systematic exploration of this simplified setting, the paper makes visible the dynamics of weight interaction that would be obscured in full-scale models.
connects: This work builds on growing research into the mechanistic behavior of neural networks and how learned representations encode multiple functions simultaneously. It extends previous work on interference in multitask learning and continual learning, where conflicting objectives can degrade performance, by isolating and examining the phenomenon at the weight level. The paper connects to broader efforts in neural network interpretability to understand what information weights encode and how different learning objectives shape parameter space. It also relates to work on feature reuse and knowledge sharing, examining the cost and constraints of packing multiple capabilities into shared weight matrices.
enables: This toy model framework could facilitate future investigation of weight interference across more complex settings and provide intuition for designing architectures or training procedures that better manage interference. Understanding interference weights mechanistically may inform approaches to multitask learning, continual learning, and knowledge distillation by revealing why certain design choices alleviate or exacerbate conflicts. The simplified setting could also enable theoretical analysis of interference phenomena, potentially leading to bounds or guarantees on performance degradation. More broadly, clearer understanding of weight interference mechanisms supports the larger interpretability agenda of making neural network behavior more transparent and controllable.
